--- 
title: "OMICs Analysis - Phylogeny training"
author: "OMICs Analysis"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
site: OMICS-Analysis/Tutorial_phylogenies
documentclass: book
# bibliography: [book.bib, packages.bib]
# url: your book url like https://pkg.yihui.org/bookdown
# cover-image: path to the social sharing image like images/cover.jpg
description: "Recetas principales para la graficación de filogenias"
# link-citations: yes
github-repo: OMICS-Analysis/Tutorial_phylogenies
---

# Introduction

These is our main training resource for phylogeny manipulation and graphic representation. We focus on the following *subjects*

+ NEWICK FORMAT: Content and meaning
+ Usage of the main PACKAGES for phylogeny analysis
+ RECIPES for frequent tasks

*Specific tasks*

- [x] Newick format mini-manual
- [ ] Phylogenies mini-manual
  - [ ] Distances
  - [ ] Bootstrapping and support values
  - [ ] Root
- [ ] Graph: ggtree (graphs) and its main functions
- [x] Manipulate phylogeny: treeio
- [ ] Metadata managing: df o treeio-> ggtree
- [ ] Frequent processes in visualization
 - [x] Tip and node labels
 - [x] Metadata addition in tips and nodes
 - [x] Basic functions of ggtree
 - [x] Root and re-root (first: basic concepts)
 - [x] Rotate branches
 - [x] Cut or resize an edge
 - [x] Eliminate branches
 - [x] Cut phylogeny by internal node
 - [ ] Parent, child, ancestor and MRCA identification
- [ ] Convert treeio.R* to tree_object_handling.R: phylo, tibble, ggtree, igraph
- [ ] Create: tree_explore_and_transforme.R: ancestor, child, MCRA, siblings, root, cut, prune

 *Keep in mind* that phylogenies are created with two main goals:

 1. To verify the phylogenetic position of know strains
 2. To eliminate or re-label redundant or problematic strains
 3. To test different representations using the matetadata
 4. To deliver final phylogeny to customers

So it is important that phylogenetic distances, support values, as well as relevant data are clearly visible in the phylogeny images :)
(Big font, color: high contrast)

<!--chapter:end:index.Rmd-->

# Newick Format {#newick}

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE) 
```

Load the neccesary libraries for this section

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
library("ape") #We use this library to read the newick texts
library("ggtree")
```

Newick format is a way of representing a phylogeny (a tree) with parentheses and commas. Its basic elements are:

* Branches
* Nodes
   + Leaves: Nodes with no descendants, also colled tip points
   + Internal nodes: Nodes with descendants

A Minimal newick tree has only branches. There is a branch for each comma and each internal parenthesis pair.

```{r eval=TRUE, warning= FALSE}
#   (  , (,)  )
#        ^ ^    internal parenthesis pair.
#   ^         ^  external parenthesis
```

Lets obtain this tree in phylo format
```{r eval=TRUE, warning= FALSE}
tree<-"(,(,));" 
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
```

And now we can use ggtree to generate an image
```{r fig1, fig.cap='A minimal newick tree', out.width='50%', fig.align='center', warning=FALSE}
ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()
```

Here you can see tip points and internal nodes colored in red and blue, respectively

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
ggtree(tree)+
  geom_tippoint(size=3, color="red")+
  geom_nodepoint(size=3, color="blue")
```

The tree leaves (tip points) can be labeled 
Labels correspond to clades (species, strains)

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
tree<-"(cat,(dog,fox));" 
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()
```

You can indicate different lengths for each branch
The length reflects the amount of evolutionary change of the branch

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1):0.2);" 
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()
```

You can also label internal nodes.
Internal nodes are common ancestors for a group of leaves
```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1)Anc2:0.2)Anc1;" 
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab()
```

... or instead of a node label you can use a bootstrap value
Bootstrap values indicate the support of a given node.
Here, the event with fox and dog having a common ancestor has a confidence of 0.9 

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1)0.9:0.2)0.98;" 
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
ggtree(tree,)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab()
```

Here we have a more complex example

```{r eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
tree<-"(Bovine:0.69395,
      (Gibbon:0.36079,
          (Orang:0.33636,
              (Gorilla:0.17147,
                  (Chimp:0.19268, Human:0.11927)0.5:0.08386
               )0.9:0.06124
           )0.7:0.15057
       )0.8:0.54939,Mouse:1.21460)0.9;"
tree<-read.tree(text=tree)
ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab()
```

## APPENDIX1 1: Converting to a R phylo object (see apendix below)

All of the following functions can create a phylo object from a newick file
```{r eval=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
ape::read.tree("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class
phyloseq::read_tree("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class
phytools::read.newick("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class
treeio::read.newick("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class
```




<!--chapter:end:01-Newick_Format.Rmd-->
